title: Who Controls the AI?
subtitle: Designing the onboarding for a vibe-coding platform where AI builds databases from plain-language descriptions — and users stay in control
team: Direct collaboration with Ninox CEO and technical co-founder, pre-product-team handoff, Berlin

hero_image.alt: Ninox AI onboarding interface — user types a database description, AI generates the structure, user edits the result

sections.headline: Who Controls the AI?
sections.subtext: A founder-led stealth project to extend Ninox's reach. The platform is incredibly powerful once a schema exists — but defining the schema was the gate that kept most non-technical SMEs out. The vibe-coding approach generates a schema from a plain-language description. The design problem: how to hand a non-database-savvy user a schema they can actually own.

images.alt: Ninox AI onboarding — prompt to database flow
images.caption: Describe your database. AI generates the structure. You edit.

sections.headline: The Context
sections.content:
Ninox is a no-code business application platform — people build their own ERP systems, project trackers, case management tools and data apps without writing code. Its superpower (and its hurdle) is that it is database-first. Once a schema exists, Ninox auto-generates rich interfaces, applies direct manipulation everywhere, and the document/record mentality fits SME workflows perfectly — law firms, construction companies, contractors, inspectors, the kind of business where every job is a record with attachments and statuses.

That database-first design is also what kept Ninox's most successful customers in a narrow band: people who already had some database knowledge, or businesses that hired a consultant to set the platform up. The vibe-coding initiative was meant to extend the reach. Generate the schema from a plain-language description, and people without database training can cross the gate that previously stopped them.

I worked on this directly with Ninox's CEO and technical co-founder in a stealth pre-product-team phase — a founder-level exploration of how onboarding for this should feel. The engineering of the AI generation was being handled separately. My question was the harder one: what happens after the AI shows you what it built, when you are a non-technical SME owner who has never thought about tables and relations before?

images.alt: Ninox AI onboarding — the prompt input screen, with category suggestions and an empty preview panel
images.caption: The entry point: describe what you want to build. The right panel is empty until the AI generates something.

sections.quote: Show the output. Then let the user edit it. Never leave them staring at something they can't change.
sections.context: The core design principle that shaped the onboarding

sections.headline: Why chat alone wouldn't work
sections.content:
The obvious AI-onboarding pattern is a chat interface: user describes the database, AI replies with the schema rendered as text or a structured response, user asks for changes in chat, repeat. That pattern breaks here in two ways that aren't theoretical.

### Long chat outputs don't scale to schemas

A realistic SME database has eight or twelve tables, each with ten or twenty fields, plus relations. A chat that prints that schema back to the user — and then expects the user to type 'change the `Customer` table's `phone` field to a multi-value field, and add a `country` field after it' — is going to feel like writing migration scripts in prose. The schema is large enough that the chat transcript stops being a conversation and starts being a wall of generated text the user has to navigate.

### Trial users won't write a long spec upfront

The other end of the conversation has the same problem in reverse. A trial user is not going to type a 400-word spec into the first prompt: 'I need a CRM for my law firm with clients, matters, case files, billing records, deadlines, contact people per client, document attachments per matter…' They will type one sentence. The first AI-generated schema will be off by at least a little — sometimes by more. Recovering from that mismatch through additional chat turns leads back to the first problem.

Both constraints together meant chat alone was not a viable interaction model. The schema had to live on a surface the user could see and touch directly.

steps.label: Schema renders into the real data-model editor
steps.description: The AI doesn't produce a chat-rendered preview of the schema; it generates directly into the same data-model editor a user would touch to build a database manually. Tables appear as cards, fields are clickable, relationships are drawn as arrows. The user sees what they would normally see — just generated, not built by hand.

steps.label: Direct manipulation as the primary path; chat as the alternative
steps.description: Every table card has 'add field' and 'edit' affordances right there. Users can add fields, rename them, change types, add or remove entire tables without opening the chat. Chat stays available for changes that are easier to describe than to click — but the user picks which path matches the change they want to make. Both paths converge on the same schema.

steps.label: First-schema-is-wrong, handled gracefully
steps.description: Because the first prompt will be short and the first schema will be off, the design accepts that as the normal case. Suggested-actions chips below the chat propose obvious additions ('add SLA tracking to tickets', 'add escalation flags to agents'). The user doesn't have to remember everything they want — the AI keeps proposing, the user keeps editing, and the schema grows incrementally.

steps.label: The schema, once built, is just a Ninox app
steps.description: There is no separate 'AI mode' the user has to leave. Once the schema feels right, they click into the app and start using it — same builder, same sidebar, same edit affordances they have just used. The vibe-coding generation step folds into Ninox's normal authoring surface; it doesn't replace it.

sections.headline: The design principle behind it
sections.content:
The underlying principle isn't unique to databases. It applies to any AI-generated artefact that a human has to take ownership of.

The AI's job is to reduce the blank-screen problem. The hardest part of building a database from scratch — especially as someone who has never built one — is starting. AI solves that. But once the screen has content, the user has to feel immediately in control of it, or the AI has just moved the problem: instead of staring at a blank screen, they're staring at someone else's database.

For an SME owner — a partner at a small law firm, the dispatcher at a roofing company — the schema needs to belong to them within a few minutes of seeing it. That ownership shift is what makes the rest of Ninox's power available to them. Auto-magical interfaces only feel magical if the data model underneath is yours.

images.alt: The AI builder showing both the chat panel (with iterative edits) and the visual data model with editable table cards
images.caption: Two paths, side by side. Chat-driven iteration on the left; direct-edit affordances (‘Create field’ on every card) on the right. The user picks whichever matches the change they want to make.

images.alt: The generated schema running as a normal Ninox app, in the standard builder UI with the sidebar tables and Builder mode toggle
images.caption: After generation, the AI-built schema is just a regular Ninox app. Same builder, same sidebar, same edit affordances. There is no separate ‘AI mode’ to leave.

sections.headline: Outcome

metrics.label: Engagement scope
metrics.value: Founder-led stealth phase; design handed off to the product team as the basis for the public Ninox AI platform (beta 2026)

metrics.label: Interaction model
metrics.value: Split-screen direct manipulation alongside chat — adopted as the primary onboarding pattern

metrics.label: Audience reach
metrics.value: Designed to make Ninox usable for SMEs without database knowledge — law firms, construction, contractors, inspectors — where setup previously required a consultant

sections.narrative: The 'show the schema, let the user edit it directly' principle became the spine of the AI onboarding when the work transitioned from founder collaboration to product-team build-out. The strategic point — that direct manipulation has to coexist with chat, not be replaced by it — set the interaction model for the beta.

sections.headline: What this means for AI product design
sections.content:
Chat-only AI interfaces work well for short, completable interactions — write me an email, summarise this document, answer this question. They stop working when the artefact is structured and large enough that the user needs to see and touch it directly: a database schema, a contract draft, an org chart, a project plan.

For those cases, the design problem is the seam — how the chat output lands somewhere the user can actually own. In Ninox, that seam is the data-model editor: the AI generates into it, and the user picks up editing in it. The chat doesn't disappear; it stops being the only path. That coexistence is what made AI-assisted onboarding feasible for users who couldn't have written the spec themselves and wouldn't have managed the schema through chat.

The same seam will matter wherever AI is used to onboard non-experts into a structured artefact — legal AI included. The user needs the document, not a chat transcript about the document.

images.alt: The generated ticketing app in production use — Tickets table with colored Priority and Category chips, a record detail panel, and the full schema running as a real product
images.caption: The proof: the AI-generated schema becomes a real working product. Records, relations, and field types behave like any hand-built Ninox app.

sections.headline: Why this matters for legal AI
sections.content: Legal AI has the same structural problem Ninox had. The artefacts — contracts, case files, intake forms, schedules — are large and structured. The user is an expert in their domain but not in the AI's representation of it. A chat-only interface either explodes into walls of generated text or assumes the user can spec the whole thing upfront. The split-screen pattern — AI generates into the real editing surface, direct manipulation lives next to chat, the artefact stays touchable from the first moment — ports directly. I've designed this exact handoff in a different domain.

executive.summary: Founder-led stealth project (with the CEO and technical co-founder, pre-product-team handoff) to extend Ninox's reach beyond the database-savvy. Designed the vibe-coding onboarding: AI generates the schema from a plain-language description; chat and direct manipulation coexist; users edit tables and fields directly in the real data-model editor. Adopted as the interaction model for the beta launching 2026.
executive.angle: Direct manipulation as the seam between AI generation and user ownership

meta.confidentiality_note: Freelance consultation post-employment, in stealth phase with the CEO and technical co-founder. No confidential company data; platform is launching in public beta 2026.
meta.source: data/experiences/ninox-head-of-product-design.md + user corrections 2026-05-24 (stealth-with-founders context; SME audience specifics; two UX constraints — long-chat-outputs infeasible + trial-users-won't-spec — that drove split-screen direct manipulation)
